coredata.dc:title: Synergistic enhancement of photoluminescence and advanced deep learning model through YOLOv8x in combined effects of carbon dots and Sr₉Al₆O₁₈:Sm³⁺ phosphors

coredata.dc:description: A series of orange-red-emitting Sr₉Al₆O₁₈:Sm³⁺ (1–11 mol %) phosphors are synthesized using the combustion method, while carbon dots (CDs) are produced from urea and citric acid. The incorporation of CDs into the Sr₉Al₆O₁₈:Sm³⁺ (SAO:Sm3+) matrix reduced both negative and positive defects, enhancing the crystallinity of the phosphor. A significant spectral overlap is observed between the emission of CDs and the absorption of SAO:Sm³⁺. An 11.9-folds enhancement in luminescence intensity is detected from the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composite, along with an extended fluorescence lifetime. The energy transfer between CDs and Sm³⁺ is demonstrated, with the increased PL intensity attributed to the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism. The CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composites demonstrated excellent selectivity and high contrast when developing latent fingerprints (LFPs). Level I–III structural characteristics of LFPs are easily recognized under ulta violet (UV) light. Furthermore, YOLOv8x, an effective deep learning model, has been modified for the important forensic science task of LFPs recognition. YOLOv8x is able to precisely locate LFPs in complex backgrounds by employing the real-time object detection capabilities of the architecture. With this adjusting, detection speed and accuracy are much increased, making it an effective tool for forensic analysis and law enforcement applications. The optimized orange-red-emitting CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composites is synthesized with soluble polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to produce a transparent anti-counterfeiting ink. The resulting film exhibited excellent flexibility, foldability, and superior thermal and optical properties. In summary, the highly efficient CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composite is a versatile luminescent material with multifunctional capabilities, making it ideal for flexible films, LFPs detection, and white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs) applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of orange-red-emitting Sr₉Al₆O₁₈:Sm³⁺ (1–11 mol %) phosphors are synthesized using the combustion method, while carbon dots (CDs) are produced from urea and citric acid. The incorporation of CDs into the Sr₉Al₆O₁₈:Sm³⁺ (SAO:Sm3+) matrix reduced both negative and positive defects, enhancing the crystallinity of the phosphor. A significant spectral overlap is observed between the emission of CDs and the absorption of SAO:Sm³⁺. An 11.9-folds enhancement in luminescence intensity is detected from the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composite, along with an extended fluorescence lifetime. The energy transfer between CDs and Sm³⁺ is demonstrated, with the increased PL intensity attributed to the Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism. The CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composites demonstrated excellent selectivity and high contrast when developing latent fingerprints (LFPs). Level I–III structural characteristics of LFPs are easily recognized under ulta violet (UV) light. Furthermore, YOLOv8x, an effective deep learning model, has been modified for the important forensic science task of LFPs recognition. YOLOv8x is able to precisely locate LFPs in complex backgrounds by employing the real-time object detection capabilities of the architecture. With this adjusting, detection speed and accuracy are much increased, making it an effective tool for forensic analysis and law enforcement applications. The optimized orange-red-emitting CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composites is synthesized with soluble polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to produce a transparent anti-counterfeiting ink. The resulting film exhibited excellent flexibility, foldability, and superior thermal and optical properties. In summary, the highly efficient CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺ composite is a versatile luminescent material with multifunctional capabilities, making it ideal for flexible films, LFPs detection, and white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs) applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: w-LEDs are the leading lighting technology of the 21st century due to their superior performance [1]. However, traditional w-LEDs, which use Ce3+ doped garnet yellow phosphors on blue chips, face issues like high correlated colour temperature (CCT) and low colour rendering index (CRI, Ra) due to the lack of red emission in their spectrum [2]. These challenges can be addressed by using tri-phosphors with near-ultraviolet (n-UV) or ultraviolet (UV) chips. Therefore, there is a need to develop new red-emitting phosphors with excellent structural and optical properties [3].

Trivalent rare earth elements possess a wide array of energy levels due to the unique electronic configurations of their 4f electron orbitals and multiple transition modes. Rare earth ions, especially Eu³⁺ and Sm³⁺, are crucial in red light-emitting luminescent materials. Sm³⁺ is frequently used to create orange-red light-emitting phosphors, typically emitting in the orange to red regions of the spectrum. Sm³⁺-doped LiBaPO₄ phosphors, known for their deep orange-red emission, are produced using the solution combustion method [4]. In a similar manner, NaSrBiTeO₆:Sm³⁺ phosphors achieve a remarkable colour purity (CP) of 99.9 % and a low CCT of 1494 K [5]. The CaLa₄Ti₄O₁₅:Sm³⁺ phosphor, which emits red-orange light, exhibits impressive thermal stability, maintaining around 81.7 % of its efficiency at 423 K [6]. Additionally, the Y₂Ca₂Ga₃VO₁₂:Sm³⁺ phosphor delivers highly efficient orange emission under near-UV excitation, while La₂(MoO₄)₃:Sm³⁺ samples generate intense orange-red emission when excited at 404 nm [7–9]. The variation in emission properties underscores how different phosphor compositions and excitation wavelengths can affect the resulting luminescence. These studies demonstrate that Sm³⁺ ions serve as efficient activators for generating vivid orange and orange-red emissions.

Because of its exceptional qualities such as their intense fluorescence, low toxicity, and exceptional biocompatibility CDs have attracted a lot of interest for a variety of novel uses [10,11]. CDs with diameters ranging from 1.2 to 3.8 nm have been synthesized and shown to have excellent up-conversion and size-dependent photoluminescence (PL). The PL characteristics of CdS nanoparticles with carbon composite are improved. Additionally, silica-based hybrids of CDs and CdS nanocrystals have been developed; these exhibit brilliant white fluorescence when exposed to UV light. Consequently, there is significant interest in exploring new applications for carbon-composited semiconductor materials, particularly for achieving tunable colour emission in single-phase phosphors. The synergistic effect of non-metal materials and metal ions on phosphor emission has been infrequently explored. Therefore, we aim to examine the combined influence of CDs and the rare earth metal ion Sm³⁺ on Sr9Al6O18 phosphors [12].

In criminal cases, fingerprint analysis has consistently produced trustworthy evidence. Fingerprints (FPs) are made up of ridges that collect sweat and oily sebum, which can leave behind persistent impressions when fingers come into contact with solid surfaces. LFPs are imprints that are frequently invisible to the naked eye. Various physical and chemical techniques are employed to extract personal information from LFPs and to visualize them for criminal investigations. Photoluminescent nanomaterials and fluorescence imaging have received a lot of attention recently. For identifying LFPs on different surfaces, a variety of luminous nanomaterials have been created, including metallic nanoparticles, carbon-based nanocomposites, and silicon-based nanoparticles [13–15]. When activated by UV light, many luminescent nanomaterials in practical applications emit blue to green light. The background fluorescence of blue or green materials, such as paper, plastic, or biological materials, may affect the quality of LFP pictures that are blue or green, thereby reducing the contrast between the FPs and the substrate. Enhancing LFP image quality and reducing background self-fluorescence interference are still significant challenges. A good way to achieve excellent image quality with less background interference and more contrast is by employing luminescent nanomaterials that produce fluorescent red light [16]. This is attributed to the minimal overlap between red light and blue or green fluorescence, creating a strong demand for red fluorescent nanomaterials for visualizing LFPs. However, achieving this has proven challenging, and only a limited number of red fluorescent nanomaterials have been reported for this application. Li et al. [17] developed bright red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots using p-phenylenediamine as a precursor, which successfully visualize LFPs with clear ridge details. Meanwhile, Saif et al. [18] created a novel Eu³⁺:Y₂Ti₂O₇/SiO₂ nanomaterial with red luminescence, which also enables clear imaging of LFPs. Yang et al. [19] created hybrid nanoparticles by integrating oligomers with silica for LFP imaging, which exhibited exceptional photostability. Dispersing these nanoparticles onto silica or polymer surfaces prevents aggregation in the solid state and improves their emission efficiency. As a result, producing red-emissive nanocomposites with high emission efficiency is an effective approach for visualizing LFP images [20].

You Only Look Once version 8 (YOLO v8x) is a cutting-edge, real-time object detection system renowned for its speed and accuracy, making it highly valuable in various fields, including forensics. The YOLOv8x, an advanced iteration of the YOLO series, is optimized for enhanced accuracy and efficiency in object detection tasks. Its advanced features can significantly benefit forensic applications, assisting in crime scene analysis, evidence examination, and digital forensics. Additionally, the system offers high detection accuracy, real-time processing, advanced feature extraction, as well as scalability and flexibility [21].

Due to their wide range of possible uses, multifunctional luminous films have attracted a lot of attention lately. Their function in anti-counterfeiting measures is very important in the information security environment of today. The issue of counterfeiting has spread throughout the world, affecting country economies, consumer safety, and national security as a whole. Currently, various anti-counterfeiting methods are widely used in the market, including anti-counterfeiting paper, radio frequency identification tags, and organic dye optical anti-counterfeiting tags [22–24]. In contrast, the advancement of rare earth-activated fluorescent anti-counterfeiting films represents a new approach to multifunctional intelligent luminescent materials. These films stand out for their cost-effectiveness, adaptable design, ease of detection, strong protection, high efficiency, transparency, and environmental friendliness. Numerous scientific studies have reported progress in the development of luminescent films tailored for anti-counterfeiting applications [25].

For example, Mi et al. combined soluble PVA with Ca8ZnY(PO4)7:Eu2+ high-efficiency phosphor to create a transparent anti-counterfeiting film [26]. This film's vivid blue emission under UV light is one of its anti-counterfeiting properties. It is noteworthy as a prospective dual-purpose luminescent platform that can be used for anti-counterfeiting applications as well as high-quality coloured lighting. Similarly, Ruan et al. optimized the phosphor (Ca18Li6-3xYx(PO4)14:0.1Eu) and used PVA as the matrix to produce an inexpensive and eco-friendly luminous security ink. This ink aims to reduce the probability that precious documents, branded goods, and currency will be counterfeited [27]. PVA is chosen as the film-forming medium by both research teams, based on the previously described investigations. PVA is a widely used water-soluble polymer that is well-known for having strong hydrophilicity, high thermal stability, outstanding mechanical qualities, and notable chemical and physical stability. In addition, PVA has excellent film-forming qualities, doesn't cause harm, and is biocompatible and naturally degradable. PVA is therefore a perfect material for applications involving anti-counterfeiting [28,29].

This study successfully synthesized SAO:Sm³⁺ (1–11 mol%) nanophosphors using a combustion synthesis method. It also explores modifying the composition of CDs to improve the red emission of the SAO:Sm³⁺ phosphor. No previous studies have detailed the preparation, photometric analysis, and colorimetric analysis of Sr9Al6O18 phosphors, highlighting the uniqueness of this research. Samples are characterized using powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and PL spectrophotometry. The phase purity, luminescence, thermal stability, decay curves and quantum yield (QY) of the materials are evaluated in detail. Additionally, a novel method for LFP detection is proposed, which leverages phosphor materials to enhance visibility under specific lighting conditions. This method offers high contrast and clear identification of intricate fingerprint features, enhancing the accuracy of forensic analysis. Additionally, the advanced deep learning model YOLOv8x has been customized for detecting LFPs, which is essential in forensic science. By leveraging its real-time object detection capabilities, YOLOv8x can accurately and efficiently identify and localize LFPs, even in complex backgrounds. This work highlights the advantages of using phosphors for LFP detection, security inks, and W-LEDs.

The following chemicals are used for the preparation of Sm³⁺-doped Sr₉Al₆O₁₉ phosphor without further purification. Strontium nitrate (Sr(NO₃)₂, 99.99 %), Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O, 99.99 %), Samarium nitrate hexahydrate (Sm(NO₃)₃·6H₂O, 99.99 %), Urea (CO(NH₂)₂), 98.99 %) are procured form Sigma Aldrich, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SAO: Sm3+ (1–11 mol %) phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: Weigh out stoichiometric amounts of Sr(NO₃)₂, Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O, and Sm(NO₃)₃·6H₂O based on the desired dopant concentration (1–11 mol %) of Sm³⁺ in the Sr₉Al₆O₁₉ matrix. Dissolve these nitrates in a minimal amount of distilled water to form a homogeneous solution. Calculate the amount of urea needed based on the total amount of metal nitrates and the required stoichiometric ratio for a complete redox reaction. Urea serves as the fuel and reducing agent. Add urea to the metal nitrate solution and mix thoroughly until a homogeneous solution is obtained. Transfer the solution into a metal crucible suitable for high-temperature reactions. Place the crucible in a preheated muffle furnace at around 500 °C. The solution will undergo a self-sustained combustion reaction, producing a large amount of heat and evolving gases such as CO₂, N₂, and H₂O vapor. This process typically takes a few minutes and results in a foamy, porous product. After the reaction is complete, allow the crucible to cool to room temperature. The resulting product is a light, porous mass of Sr₉Al₆O₁₉:Sm³⁺ phosphor. Grind the product gently to obtain a fine powder. To enhance the crystallinity and remove any residual organic content, the powder can be further calcined at a higher temperature, typically around 900 °C for 3 h. The schematic representation of solution combustion synthesis of (1–11 mol %) of Sm³⁺ in the Sr₉Al₆O₁₉ phosphor is depicted in Fig. 1 a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots via micro-wave method
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesizing CDs using urea and citric acid is a simple and efficient method as shown in Fig. 1b. Mix 2 g of citric acid with 1 g of urea in a microwave-safe glass container. Add a small amount of deionized water (enough to dissolve the citric acid and urea) to form a homogeneous solution. Place the container in a microwave oven. Heat the mixture at 600 W for 5 min. The solution will undergo a rapid thermal reaction, resulting in dehydration, carbonization and polymerization of the precursors. As the reaction proceeds, the mixture will change colour, typically turning dark brown indicating the formation of CDs. Carefully remove the container from the microwave and allow it to cool to room temperature. Add deionized water to the cooled product to dissolve the CDs. The resulting solution is centrifuge followed by filtration through a membrane filter (0.22 μm) to remove larger particles and unreacted precursors. Further, purify the solution by dialyzing it against deionized water for 24 h to remove any remaining small molecules or impurities. The obtained CDs have further lyophilized to remove the moisture content and obtained powder stored at RT for characterization and applications These CDs typically exhibit strong fluorescence under UV light and are useful for applications such as bioimaging, sensors, and light-emitting devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of carbon dots into Sr₉Al₆O₁₉³⁺ phosphor
ce:section.ce:para: Slowly add the CDs to the Sr₉Al₆O₁₉:Sm³⁺ phosphor containing 5 mL of DI water in a beaker. Stir the mixture thoroughly to ensure uniform distribution of the CDs within the phosphor matrix. The obtained mixture is subjected to probe sonication for 30 min. Evaporate the water by heating the mixture at 80 °C for 2 h until completely dry. Ensure that the CDs are well integrated into the phosphor matrix without agglomeration. Fig. 1c shows the fabrication procedure of Sr₉Al₆O₁₉:Sm³⁺@CDs composite.

A healthy donor under the age of 25 years old provides LFPs. The LFPs are collected from a variety of surfaces, such as glass, OHP sheets, and leaves. Before being collected, donors should allow at least 10 min to wash their hands. Hands should be well cleaned with soap before being collected, and they should then be gently dried with a paper towel. Donors are directed to press their fingertips against the surface in order to capture a LFP impression. Before processing, finger prints (FPs) that are produced are stored for at least 12 h at room temperature (20–30 °C) in a light-free environment. The 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ NCs is carefully dusted onto the surfaces of the substrates with FPs. Next, the area where the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ NCs have been applied is exposed to 365 nm UV light, and photos of the LFPs are captured using a digital camera (DSLR, 5 mm focal length).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PVA/CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composite film
ce:section.ce:para: First, deionized water is used to dissolve a predetermined amount of PVA; typical Mw ≈ 70,000–80,000, 98–99 mol% alcoholized). An 8 wt% PVA translucent solution is created after dissolving with stirring at 80 °C. To create the PVA/CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite film security ink, the necessary amount of CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ NCs dispersion is then added to these 8 wt% PVA solutions, and the combination is stirring for 90 min. After that, the mixture is processed ultrasonically for 60 min to make sure the CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite is distributed evenly throughout the 8 wt% PVA solution. The anti-counterfeiting ink is then poured into a Petri dish and allowed to air dry for 24 h in a vacuum oven, resulting in a dry, transparent anti-counterfeiting film. The preparation process for the PVA/CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ NC film is illustrated in Fig. 1d.

The W-LED device is fabricated using optimized SAO:7Sm³⁺ orange-red phosphors, commercially available BAM:Eu2 ⁺ blue phosphors, (Ba, Sr)₂SiO₄:Eu2 ⁺ green phosphors, and a 395 nm near-ultraviolet chip sourced from Shenzhen Looking Long Technology Co., Ltd. Firstly, the organic silica gel is properly mixed with the phosphors. After that, a 395 nm chip is covered with this a paste, and it is let to solidify. After 1 h of drying at 120 °C, electroluminescence tests are run on the constructed device using drive currents ranging from 20 mA to 120 mA.

This study employs various tools to evaluate and analyze the data. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of the synthesized materials are obtained using a Shimadzu diffractometer (CuKα = 1.5406 Å, 45 kV, 200 mA). Adjusting the 2θ range between 10° and 80° results in a scanning rate of 2°/min. Rietveld refinement, conducted using FullProof software, is employed to analyze how different concentrations of Sm³⁺ doping affect the cubic perovskite structure, as shown by the PXRD profiles. XPS is conducted using an Esca lab 250Xi spectrometer. The microscopic structure and elemental composition are examined using a JEOL-JEM-2100 PLUS FE-SEM and an EDAX Apollo XT EDS system. Raman spectroscopy is performed using a micro-Raman spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. PL analysis is conducted using a Horiba spectrofluorometer equipped with a 450 W Xenon lamp to record the excitation and emission spectra at room temperature. The FluoroLog 3-TCSPC system is employed to collect PL lifetime data and to determine the QY utilizing an integrating sphere.

Fig. 2 a displays the PXRD patterns of SAO:Sm³⁺ (1–11 mol%) phosphors, including their respective Miller (hkl) planes. These patterns are well-matched with the standard cards JCPDS #79–1094 and ICSD #066062 [30], indicating that they correspond to the Sr9Al6O18 phase of the cubic perovskite system with the Pa
3 ‾
(205) space group and 2/m
3 ‾
point group. Even with the addition of Sm³⁺ ions, no additional peaks are found in the scanning range of 10–80°, indicating the creation of a pure crystalline phase of the Sr9Al6O18 host matrix. Furthermore, the PXRD patter of prepared CDs (Fig. 2a) exhibits a large hump at a 2θ value of about 20°, which corresponds to the amorphous carbon (002) planar characteristic. Additionally, Fig. 2a displays the XRD pattern of 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite, where the prominent peak at 2θ ≈ 20° indicates the incorporation of CDs into the phosphor matrix. An enlarged view of the peak at 31.98° is shown in Fig. 2b, corresponding to the (440) plane. The consistent peak positions suggest that Sr³⁺ ions have been successfully substituted by Sm³⁺ ions, which are of similar size [31].

To examine the detailed changes in phase structure, Rietveld refinement is performed using GSAS software [32] to refine the phase structures of both the SAO matrix and the Sm³⁺ doped SAO phosphor. The diffraction peaks for both systems are precisely fitted using the refinement model with the crystallographic information file (CIF) COD No. 1008450 [33], as shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. S1a. The Bragg's parameters, detailed in Table 1 , fall within 10 %, indicating that the refinement is successful. The profiles are accurately refined using the Pseudo-Voigt function.

Additionally, by using Vesta software to analyze the data from the refining approach which is provided as a CIF file, the 3D crystal structure of Sr₉Al₆O₁₈ powder and the coordination environment of the cationic sites within the unit cell can be detected [34]. With the structural constants α = β = γ = 90°, Z = 8, and a = b = c = 15.809 Å, this is shown in Fig. 2d. The Sr2⁺ ions are dispersed across many polyhedral units: Sr1, Sr2, and Sr3 are found in SrO₆ octahedral units, whereas Sr4, Sr5, and Sr6 have coordination numbers of 9, 8, and 7, respectively (Fig. 2e). Notably, all Al³⁺ ions are observed to be in tetrahedral units with a coordination number of 4 (Fig. 2f). Table 1 compares the unit cell parameters of the SAO host with those of the SAO:Sm³⁺ phosphor. These properties are significantly affected by the introduction of Sm3 ⁺ ions at different concentrations. As an illustration, the doped materials volume dropped from 3964.56 Å³ to 3951.05 Å³. The following formula is used to calculate the absolute displacement of cations by activator ions [35].:

The coordination numbers and effective ionic radii of the host and activator cations are denoted by Rh (CN) and Rd (CN), respectively, in the equation. Simplifying further gives a Δr of 51.11 % between Al³⁺ (ionic radius, r = 67.5 p.m.) and Sm³⁺ (r = 102 p.m.), whereas Δr of 15.70 % (within the acceptable range of less than 30 %) is found between Sr2 ⁺ (r = 121 p.m.) and Sm³⁺. This confirms the successful substitution of Sr2 ⁺ ions with Sm³⁺ ions in the solid solution. The key refined parameters, including lattice constants and bond lengths, are detailed in Tables S1 and S2. Additionally, the average crystallite sizes of the synthesized nanomaterials are calculated using the Williamson-Hall fitting method, following Scherrer's equation as described in Ref. [36].

In this equation, θ represents the diffraction angle, D is the average crystallite size, λ is the X-ray wavelength (1.545 Å), and η denotes the micro-strain. B₀ (2θ) and Bₛᵢ (2θ) correspond to the full width at half-maximum (FWHM, in radians) of the most intense peak for the Sm³⁺ doped phosphor. The estimated crystallite size (D) values are listed in Table 2 . The table shows that as the Sm³⁺ concentration increases, the D values decrease, while dislocation density (δ), micro-strain (ε), and stacking fault values increase (Fig. S2). The decrease in crystallite size (D) with increasing Sm³⁺ concentration can be attributed to the following mechanisms: (i) the incorporation of Sm³⁺ ions into the crystal lattice introduces additional dislocations, which are line defects that disrupt the regular atomic arrangement. These dislocations act as barriers to the movement of atoms, hindering crystal growth and resulting in smaller crystallites. (ii) Sm³⁺ doping induces lattice distortions due to the size mismatch between the Sm³⁺ ions and the host cations. This mismatch creates internal stress or micro-strain within the lattice, which impedes the coalescence of small crystallites into larger ones. (iii) The addition of Sm³⁺ ions increase the likelihood of stacking faults, which are planar defects where the normal sequence of atomic planes is disrupted. These faults further disrupt the crystal structure, creating additional nucleation sites for crystallite formation and preventing the growth of larger crystallites [37]. Together, these defects increase the internal energy of the system, making it energetically more favorable to form smaller crystallites. Similarly, the crystallite sizes for the series of developed phosphors are calculated using the W–H plot method, as shown in Fig. S1b.

The FE-SEM images of SAO phosphors doped with varying concentrations of Sm³⁺ ions (1–11 mol%) reveal that the particles predominantly exhibit a spherical morphology (Fig. 3 a–g). The particles are well-defined with minimal agglomeration, suggesting a uniform distribution of the Sm³⁺ dopant within the SAO matrix. The spherical shape of the particles indicates effective control over the synthesis process, promoting consistent growth and surface energy minimization. Additionally, the low level of agglomeration can be attributed to the optimized synthesis parameters, which prevent excessive particle coalescence, thereby maintaining distinct particle boundaries. As the Sm³⁺ concentration increases, no significant variation in particle shape or size distribution is observed, confirming the stability of the phosphor's structural properties across the doping range. The FE-SEM images of the synthesized CDs reveal that the particles are predominantly spherical in shape, with uniform size distribution (Fig. 3h). The spherical morphology suggests that the CDs are successfully formed under controlled synthesis conditions, leading to consistent particle formation.

The smooth surface and well-defined edges of the particles further indicate minimal aggregation and high monodispersity. The FE-SEM images of the CDs integrated into the phosphor matrix reveal a homogeneous distribution of spherical CDs within the phosphor material (Fig. 3i). The CDs maintain their characteristic spherical morphology, with minimal aggregation observed, indicating effective dispersion within the matrix. The integration of CDs into the phosphor does not disrupt the overall structure of the phosphor, as both components exhibit well-defined and distinct particle boundaries. This uniform distribution and compatibility between the CDs and the phosphor matrix are essential for enhancing the composite material's optical properties and performance. The particle size distribution histogram of phosphor and CDs in 5 wt%CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composites are plotted using ImageJ software [38] indicates a relatively narrow size distribution, with the majority of the phosphor cantered around an average size of 0.130 mm (Fig. 3j) and the size of embedded CDs is between 2 and 5 nm, with an average size of 3.73 nm (Fig. 3k). The histogram reveals that the particles are predominantly within this size range, highlighting the uniformity and consistency achieved in the synthesis process. The narrow distribution further suggests that the doping process does not significantly affect the particle size, maintaining a well-controlled and reproducible particle morphology essential for the material's optical properties.

As seen in Fig. 4 a, TEM is used to examine the CDs microscopic features. The analysis indicates that the particles are spherical, homogeneously monodispersed, and without of aggregation. Their small size is further confirmed by the particle size range of 2–5 nm and narrow size distribution. Fig. 4b presents the TEM image of SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor, which clearly shows nearly uniform-sized particles with minimal agglomeration, with particle sizes ranging from 40 to 60 nm. The TEM image of the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite is shown in Fig. 4c. The integration of CDs into the phosphor is clearly visible, with the size of the CDs ranging from 3 to 5 nm, while the phosphor particles are observed to be between 40 and 60 nm. Additionally, particle size distribution histograms for the CDs and phosphor particles in the composite are presented in Fig. 4d and e. The histograms clearly show that the average particle sizes of the CDs and SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor are approximately 3.74 nm and 47.42 nm, respectively, confirming the successful integration of CDs into the phosphor.

The HR-TEM images of CDs, SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor, and the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite are shown in Fig. 4f–h. High crystallinity is indicated by the significant lattice fringes that are observable in the images, with an inter-planar distance (d) of 0.342 nm matching to the (002) plane of the CDs (Fig. 4f). For the (400) plane, the average d value of the phosphor is determined to be 0.279 nm (Fig. 4g). Furthermore, Fig. 4h displays the composite's HR-TEM image. The graph displays the estimated d values of the CDs and phosphor, which are 0.279 nm for the (002) plane and 0.341 nm for the (400) plane, respectively. Additionally, the lattice spacing (d) is estimated using GATAN software [39], and the corresponding values closely match the HR-TEM results (Fig. 4i–l).

Fig. 5 a and c shows the EDS spectra of SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite. The spectra confirm the presence of Sr, Al, O, Sm, and C elements in the surface scanning area of both the SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite. Fig. 5b and d presents the elemental overlay images of the phosphor and composite, demonstrating that Sr, Al, O, Sm, and C are evenly distributed across the surface of each material. This confirms the uniform distribution of all elements, including Sm³⁺ and C, throughout the surface, verifying the successful incorporation of Sm³⁺ and C ions into the SAO host material.

XPS is used to analyze the surface chemistry and electronic states of SAO, SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor, and the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite. The XPS spectra are obtained to identify the elemental composition, chemical states, and bonding environment of the constituent elements. Fig. 6 a displays the survey spectra of SAO, SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor, and the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite. The survey spectrum of the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite reveals core-level peaks corresponding to Sr, Al, O, Sm, and C. The observed peaks confirm the successful incorporation of Sm³⁺ ions into the Sr₉Al₆O₁₈ matrix. No significant peaks associated with impurities are detected, indicating that all the samples are of high purity. The Sr 3d spectrum shows two distinct peaks corresponding to the Sr 3d₅/₂ and Sr 3d₃/₂ spin-orbit components (Fig. 6b), centered at binding energies typically around 132.75 eV and 134.43 eV, respectively [40]. The binding energies indicate the presence of Sr2 ⁺ ions in the sample, consistent with the Sr ions in all the samples lattice. The Al 2p spectrum shows a peak centered around 73.63 eV (Fig. 6c), characteristic of Al³⁺ in an Al–O environment, confirming the presence of aluminum in the AlO₄ tetrahedra within the Sr₉Al₆O₁₈ structure [41]. The O 1s spectrum shows a peak around 531.22 eV [42], which is attributed to oxygen within the metal-oxide lattice (Fig. 6d). This binding energy suggests the presence of O2 ⁻ ions in the crystalline structure, linked to both Sr–O and Al–O bonds [43].

The Sm 3d core-level spectrum exhibits two distinct peaks corresponding to the Sm 3d₅/₂ and Sm 3d₃/₂ spin-orbit components, typically positioned at around 1082.93 eV and 1110.68 eV [44] (Fig. 6e). The binding energies correspond with the Sm³⁺ oxidation state, indicating that Sm in the doped phosphor has a 3+ state. Samarium is mostly in the +3 oxidation state within the Sr₉Al₆O₁₈ host lattice, as shown by the deconvolution of the Sm 3d spectrum, which shows the existence of Sm³⁺ ions with no detectable contributions from other oxidation states (such as Sm2 ⁺ or Sm⁴⁺) [45]. The Sr 3d, Al 2p, and O 1s spectra correspond to the expected chemical states of Sr2 ⁺, Al³⁺, and O2 ⁻, respectively, further confirming the stability and integrity of the Sr₉Al₆O₁₈ structure after doping with Sm³⁺ ions. The XPS analysis confirms the successful doping of Sr₉Al₆O₁₈ with Sm³⁺ ions, indicating that the samarium ions occupy the correct lattice sites within the host structure. The binding energies and chemical states observed in the XPS spectra match the expected composition and oxidation states, demonstrating that Sm³⁺ is effectively incorporated into the SAO matrix without causing significant alterations to the host lattice.

The PLE spectrum of SAO:7Sm³⁺, determined at λemi = 645 nm, is displayed in Fig. 7 a. A weak, broad absorption band extending from 305 to 333 nm is visible in the spectrum, peak at 319 nm, which is the charge transfer band (CTB) for O2⁻→Sm³⁺ [46]. Due to the 4f-4f transitions of Sm³⁺ ions, a series of sharp peaks appear between 340 and 500 nm, including those at 344 nm (6H₅/₂→4H₉/₂), 362 nm (6H₅/₂→4D₃/₂), 377 nm (6H₅/₂→4D₁/₂), 404 nm (6H₅/₂→4F₇/₂), 417 nm (6H₅/₂→4M₁₉/₂), 439 nm (6H₅/₂→4G₉/₂), 467 nm (6H₅/₂→4I₁₁/₂), and 484 nm (6H₅/₂→4M₁₅/₂) [47]. Among these, the peak at 404 nm in the n-UV region stands out as the most prominent and is regarded as the optimal wavelength for phosphor excitation. Fig. 7b shows the emission spectra of SAO:7Sm³⁺ under different excitation wavelengths, clearly indicating that the emission intensity at 404 nm is considerably higher than at other wavelengths. The integral intensity for excitation at 404 nm is found to be 3.3 times higher than for other excitation wavelengths, according to calculations. In addition, the 4G₅/₂→6H₉/₂ transition produces the strongest peak at 645 nm. Other distinctive transitions of Sm³⁺ ions, including the 4G₅/₂→6Hᵢ/₂ transitions, where i = 5, 7, and 11, are responsible for emissions at 563, 601, and 707 nm [48]. It is crucial to understand that these emission peaks require both magnetic dipole (MD) and electric dipole (ED) transitions. The peaks at 563 and 645 nm correspond to pure MD and pure ED transitions, respectively, based on the selection rules. A mixture of partial MD and partial ED transitions produce the peak at 601 nm. Peaks resulting from MD transitions are less susceptible to the environmental symmetry surrounding Sm³⁺ in the SAO host than are those originating from ED transitions, according to Judd-Ofelt theory. The theory also provides a parameter (R) to measure the asymmetry of the Sm³⁺ ion sites in the SAO matrix. R is the ratio of the MD transitions emission peak intensities to the ED transitions [49].

For SAO:7Sm³⁺, R = 6.32 > 1, indicating that the ED transitions are more intense than the MD transitions. This is consistent with the earlier structural analysis and implies that the Sm³⁺ ions occupy asymmetric positions within the lattice. The 4G₅/₂→6H₇/₂ transition is generally responsible for the greatest emission around 601 nm observed in Sm³⁺-doped inorganic phosphors occupying symmetrical sites. Examples include YCa₄O(BO₃)₃:Sm³⁺ (600 nm), GdAl₃(BO₃)₄:Sm³⁺ (600 nm), and BaSi₂O₅:Sm³⁺ (598 nm) [50–52]. In contrast, phosphors located at asymmetrical positions exhibit their strongest emission around 645 nm (4G₅/₂→ 6H₉/₂), which is more suitable for emitting light that matches the absorption peak of plant pigment PR (∼660 nm). The energy-level diagram of Sm³⁺ ions in Fig. 7c provides an effective representation of the excitation and emission mechanisms of SAO:7Sm³⁺. Electrons in the ground state 6H₅/₂ absorb energy and change into the excited state 4F₇/₂ when excited at 404 nm. These electrons then relax non-radiatively to the 4G₅/₂ state. Shortly thereafter, they return to the 6H₅/₂, 6H₇/₂, 6H₉/₂, and 6H₁₁/₂ levels, emitting orange-red light through radiative transitions [53].

The effects of different Sm³⁺ concentrations (1–11 mol %) on SAO phosphor emission are displayed in Fig. 7d. Up to a doping level of 7 %, the intensity gradually increases while the peak's position and shape remain constant as concentration rises. The normalized intensity displayed in Fig. 7e clearly illustrates how the intensity starts to decrease beyond this concentration. This observation suggests that this phosphor is undergoing concentration quenching [54]. Usually, the quenching mechanism is found using the critical energy transfer distance (Rc). The quenching corresponds to exchange interacts with if Rc ≤ 5 Å, and to multipole-multipole interactions otherwise. Using the Blasse's equation, the Rc value for the SAO:Sm³⁺ phosphors is determined [55].:

The formula specifies the ideal concentration of dopant Sm3⁺ ions as Xc = 0.07, while V and N stand for the unit cell volume and the number of chemical units per unit cell, respectively (V = 3951.05 Å³ and N = 2). Because of this, the estimated Rc value of 36.67 Å is much more than the usual value of 5 Å, indicating that multipole-multipole interactions are the cause of the quenching incident in this system. Here, K and β stand for constants, I is the emission spectrum's integral intensity, and x is the Sm³⁺ ion concentration. Multipole interactions can be classified as nearest-neighbour ions (θ = 3), dipole-dipole interactions (θ = 6), dipole-quadrupole interactions (θ = 8), or quadrupole-quadrupole interactions (θ = 10), depending on the parameter. The inset of Fig. 7e shows the linear fitting of log(I/x) versus log(x), yielding a θ value of 5.49, which is close to 6, and a slope of −1.83. Based on a recent study, this suggests that dipole-dipole interactions are responsible for the concentration quenching observed in SAO:Sm³⁺ phosphors [58].

The PL spectra of prepared CDs are shown in Fig. 8 a. A broad band with a peak at 503 nm is seen in the emission spectra, and a broad band with a peak at 423 nm is shown in the excitation spectrum. These luminescence properties are characteristic of CDs, with the broad emission and excitation bands mainly ascribed to the CD surface states. When compared to the SAO:7Sm³⁺ nanophosphor, the emission intensity of 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite is increased by a factor of 11.9 (Fig. 8b). The PL intensity of this material is compared with several different composites as provided in Table 3 [59–63]. Furthermore, for all employed excitation wavelengths, the integrated PL intensity of 5 wt % CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite is noticeably higher than that of SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor (Fig. 8c). FRET between the Sm³⁺ ions and the CDs is responsible for this increase [64]. The spectral overlap between the SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphors absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum of the CDs is the factor that enables FRET effective.

For FRET to occur efficiently, the emission peaks of the CDs must align well with the absorption peaks of the phosphor. When this spectral overlap is significant, energy from the excited CDs can be transferred to the Sm³⁺ ions. Upon excitation, the CDs emit light at specific wavelengths. If these wavelengths align with the absorption band of the SAO:7Sm³⁺, energy can be transferred from the CDs to the Sm³⁺ ions. This transfer is a non-radiative process mediated through d-d interactions [65]. The efficiency of FRET is inversely proportional to the sixth power of the distance between the donor and acceptor. Therefore, for effective FRET, the CDs and Sm³⁺ ions need to be in close proximity. In this case, incorporating CDs with SAO:7Sm³⁺ can enable this close interaction if the composite is properly engineered. The excitation of CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ at 423 nm can provide insights into the mechanism behind the luminescence enhancement. Consequently, Sm³⁺ electrons undergo a shift from the 4G₅/₂ state to different levels of excited energy states. In the CDs, electrons undergo a simultaneous transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) as a result of the absorbed photon energy (Fig. 8d). The electrons use the FRET process to transfer energy to the Sm³⁺ levels after relaxing to their surface and core state energies following excitation [66]. Orange-red light is released as a result of the electrons' non-radiative relaxation transition to higher energy states and subsequent return to the ground states of Sm³⁺ (6H₉/₂, 6H₆/₂, and 6H₅/₂).

The CIE chromaticity coordinates, CP, and CCT serve as essential parameters for evaluating a phosphor's luminous efficacy. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of SAO:Sm³⁺ phosphor with 1–11 mol % doping are shown in Fig. 8e. The chromaticity coordinates very slightly vary, as Fig. 8f illustrates. The minimal shift in CIE coordinates, with Sx 2 = 0.00264 and Sy 2 = 0.00316, indicates that the phosphors exhibit good colour stability. Moreover, the CP is determined by the distance between the equal energy point (xi, yi) (0.333, 0.333) and the chromaticity coordinates (xs, ys) of the phosphor [67]:

The optimized SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and the 5 wt % CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite exhibit CP of 95.2 % and 97.9 %, respectively. CCT can be calculated using the empirical McCamy formula [68]:

where n=(x-xe)/(y-ye), xe = 0.332, ye = 0.186, and (x, y) refers to the chromaticity coordinates of the samples. The average CCT values for the optimized SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite are 1500 K and 1521 K, respectively. Table 4 provide details on the CIE chromaticity coordinates, CP, and CCT for the optimized SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite. The SAO:Sm³⁺ phosphors exhibit high colour stability, high CP, and low CCT, making them well-suited for use as commercial red phosphors in white LEDs.

The τ values are measured as 0.6723 ms for SAO:7Sm³⁺, 1.014 ms for CDs, and 0.928 ms for 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ NCs. The extended lifetime observed for 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite, compared to SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor, suggests that FRET is occurring between the Sm³⁺ ions and the CDs.

The internal quantum efficiency (I
QE ) of the nanoparticles is essential for evaluating their effectiveness and suitability for application in w-LEDs. Studies comparing the emission spectra of SAO:7Sm³⁺ NPs and 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ NCs with the excitation line of the BaSO₄ reference are shown in Fig. 9b and c. One can compute the I

Here, ER represents the emission light spectrum of BaSO₄, ES denotes the integrated intensity of the phosphor, LS is the emission spectrum of the phosphor, and η signifies the IQE value. Fig. 9b and c shows the IQE graphs for SAO:7Sm³⁺ and 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composites, with calculated IQE values of 72.4 % and 78.3 %, respectively. This outcome validates the phosphor strong luminescence performance. As shown in Fig. 9d, the SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composites are evaluated at 404 nm excitation and compared to other orange-red emitting phosphors. The 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composites exhibit superior quantum efficiency when compared to other reported literatures (Table 5 ) [72–77]. These findings confirm the suitability of 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composites as a material for w-LEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of 5 wt %CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composites in w-LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electroluminescence spectra of red LEDs packaged with SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composites are displayed in Fig. 10 a and b. A blue-ray chip at 405 nm, which is close to its excitation wavelength [78], is coated with the composite. The electroluminescence spectrum, which has about four peaks, closely approaches the PL spectrum of the SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor. The LED emits brilliant red light. The transitions 4G₅/₂→6H₅/₂, 4G₅/₂→6H₇/₂, 4G₅/₂→6H₉/₂, and 4G₅/₂→6H₁₁/₂ are represented by these peaks. The image of the red LED and its CIE colour coordinates are displayed in Fig. 10b. The colour of the red LED is most similar to the phosphor at (0.598, 0.384). An orange-red phosphor (SAO:7Sm³⁺), a green phosphor ((Ba, Sr)₂SiO₄:Eu2⁺), and a blue phosphor (BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu2 ⁺) are mixed and applied to a 405 nm n-UV chip to make a w-LED [79]. The LEDs electroluminescence spectrum is displayed in Fig. 10c. There is an excellent match in luminescent colour and CIE location as the w-LEDs CIE coordinates (0.357, 0.376) are near the typical white point (0.333, 0.333). The LED has a colour temperature of 4685 K. CRI is one important performance metric for w-LEDs. The packaged w-LEDs CRI, Ra = 92, is greater than the commercial w-LEDs CRI of Ra = 80, Fig. 10d. Red light emission is efficiently increased by the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composite, leading to a significant rise in red light representation (R9). According to these results, applying the 5 wt % CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composite would be a beneficial development for the w-LED industry.

Fingerprint ridge details are often grouped into three levels, with each providing critical information for personal identification. Level I detail relates to the overall pattern type, whereas Levels I and III include elements specific to the individual. Although only the Level-1 detail may be inaccurate, the Level-II and Level-III elements are more important for identifying individuals [80–84]. Fig. 11 a and c shows FPs stained with SAO:7Sm³⁺ phosphor and a 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite on aluminum foil, both viewed in daylight. The powder appears to stick effectively to the fingerprint ridges. When exposed to near-UV radiation, the ridgelines emit red light while the grooves stay black, allowing for a clear view of the ridge patterns in fingerprint photos. Fig. 11b and d exhibit extracted fingerprint features that include not only Level-I details (such as loops and ridge ends), but also Level-II (islands and bifurcations) and Level-III details (scars and sweat pores). The increased clarity of fingerprint features at all levels is due to the great sensitivity of the best phosphor powders [85,86].

The ideal 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite successfully detects LFPs on various substrates, including porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces. Fig. 12 a and b shows that high-resolution red fluorescence pictures of LFPs on leaf, OHP, and glass can be taken using 365 nm light irradiation without the need for post-treatment. The fingerprint structure is clearly visible in these fluorescence pictures, indicating the composite's high adhesion and selectivity. The granules are evenly scattered across the FPs, but they preferentially attach to the ridges rather than the furrows, resulting in a strong orange-red fluorescence along the ridgelines. The 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite enhances fingerprint images by better interacting with ridges. Furthermore, the bright orange-red fluorescence effectively reduces background interference, such as UV light reflections on surfaces [87,88]. Fig. 12c Presents the pixel profiles of the fingerprints collected from each surface type. Each line graph corresponds to the fingerprint captured from the respective surface type, indicating variations in the fingerprint patterns. Fig. 12d shows the 3D plots of the fingerprints, visually illustrating the ridge patterns on the different surfaces. These plots represent how the fingerprints topography varies across the surfaces analyzed.

In real-world crime investigations, fingerprint remains are frequently weathered rather than freshly placed. Fig. 13 a demonstrates how the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite effectively detects LFPs on steel surfaces even after 180 days of deterioration. The fluorescent signals in fingerprint photographs steadily diminish over time. While complex ridge patterns at levels I-III can be detected for up to 120 days, level-II and level-III details become less clear in FPs after 180 days due to the loss of skin fluids and components. After 180 days of aging, the level-III ridge details in fingerprint photos fade due to the spread of remaining grease, which obscures finer features such as sweat pores. However, even after lengthy aging, level-II ridge details remain clearly apparent and can be used for identification. The 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite effectively visualizes LFPs, according to the findings. To evaluate the effectiveness of this powder composition, depletion experiments are conducted using glass slides, which are notorious for displaying FPs clearly. This study assesses the performance of CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ (5 wt%) composites in detecting low amounts of LFP residues. The identical fingerprint is pressed five times on a glass slide without recharging between imprints. Fig. 13b shows the first and fifth FPs produced during these examinations. In this sequence, the residue concentration is highest in the first imprint and lowest in the fifth. Despite the decrease in residue concentration, the quality of the produced FPs is consistently high. CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ (5 wt%) composites effectively expose ridge details under UV-365 nm light, resulting in distinct and identifiable prints. Photostability of FPs under intense UV light refers to the fingerprint's capacity to remain clear and undamaged after prolonged exposure to strong ultraviolet radiation. It is critical to assess how effectively fingerprint patterns, created with phosphors or other detection technologies, resist degradation or detail loss under UV radiation. For example, when phosphors or powders are used to disclose FPs, their photostability impacts how well the prints preserve their quality and visibility after being exposed to intense UV light. This trait is critical for situations in which FPs are studied or recorded under UV light, guaranteeing that the prints remain accurate and useful even after prolonged exposure. FPs are subjected to a UV light (36 mW/m2) for 0–180 min, as shown in Fig. 13c. The photographs show that level I-III characteristics can be easily seen for up to 150 min of UV exposure before the intensity fades.

To preserve the integrity and clarity of fingerprint evidence, substrates should not be exposed to high temperatures, and thermal conditions should be carefully considered when doing forensic analysis. Temperature can have a considerable impact on the production and retention of LFPs on metal substrates, especially when subjected to temperatures between 25 and 250 °C (Fig. 13d). Understanding these effects is critical for forensic analysis since temperature changes can impact both the quality of the fingerprint and the integrity of the metal substrate. Organic leftovers from FPs, such as oils, salts, and other chemicals, begin to degrade at temperatures between 150 and 200 °C. Although this decomposition is mild, it can result in a loss of fine details and contrast in the formed FPs. As the temperature rises, the breakdown of organic components in fingerprint remnants accelerates, resulting in more visible degradation of the prints. At 250 °C, the degradation is more severe, diminishing clarity and making small features less noticeable. Prolonged contact to temperatures above 250 °C can result in considerable thermal degradation of fingerprint remains. Because organic compounds might be completely volatilized or degraded during this considerable deterioration, fingerprint visibility is frequently lost. Furthermore, heating the metal surface to 250 °C might cause it to expand, changing the texture and finish. These modifications can influence how fingerprint residues interact with the substrate, perhaps resulting in a loss of detail or distortion of the generated prints. Prolonged exposure to high temperatures can also cause oxidation of the metal surface, resulting in a coating that obscures or modifies the look of the FPs, complicating the analysis [89].

Pixel profiles of certain places on fingerprint ridges are essential for analysing and improving fingerprint details. These profiles allow for a detailed evaluation of intensity variations and roughness along the ridges, which aids in the analysis of ridge patterns, thickness, and spacing. Pixel profiles can help us understand ridge structure, such as width, continuity, and abnormalities [90]. This detailed information is required for proper fingerprint analysis and recognition. Pixel profiles enable fingerprint comparison and matching by providing exact data on ridge patterns and features, which is critical for tasks such as fingerprint identification, forensic investigation, and biometric applications. Fig. 13a1–d1 shows the pixel profiles of chosen locations (highlighted with yellow strips) on FPs treated to various conditions (aging, subsequent FPs, UV treatment, and heat treatment). The grayscale fingerprint images reveal that pixel profiles along ridges have greater intensity values (lighter shades), whereas valleys have lower intensity values (darker shades). These findings show that this technology provides useful insights into fingerprint texture and structure, boosting the effectiveness of forensic and biometric systems [91].

The YOLOv8x deep learning algorithm is becoming increasingly popular as a real-time object detector because of its high accuracy, speed, and robustness. It provides rapid detection at over 60 frames per second, ensuring effective real-time performance with the YOLO detector. Furthermore, YOLO uses far less computer hardware than other deep CNN detectors. In this study, we used the YOLOv8x technology, the latest version of the YOLO detector, to detect fingerprint features. To improve detection performance, we constructed a specialized dataset of several fingerprint types using a 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composite and annotated with bounding boxes. We subsequently enhanced the YOLOv8x network to detect small details including ridge ends, bifurcations, loops, and scars (Fig. S3). The network learns to comprehend the patterns and features of fingerprint minutiae by being fed an enormous amount of fingerprint pictures and comments during the training process. The YOLOv8x models are trained using ReLU functions and sigmoid activation over a period of 90 epochs. 32 GB of RAM and an Intel Core TM I5-3470 CPU power a V100 server that hosts the deep learning models. Using the inexpensive Orange Pi 5 Plus, a single-board computer with an 8-core, 64-bit Rockchip CPU, we are among the first users of YOLOv8x. This CPU is created using 8 nm technology and comes in quad-core A76 and quad-core A55 variants. YOLOv8x installation for the Orange Pi 5 Plus consists of multiple phases, including configuring the development environment and constructing the model. Here's a general overview of the process.
1
Prepare the Environment:

Ensure that your Orange Pi 5 Plus is properly installed with an appropriate operating system (such as Armbian or Ubuntu). Install key dependencies such as Python, Git, and development tools.
2
Install Deep Learning Frameworks:

Install the appropriate deep learning framework and its dependencies according on your preferred framework (for example, TensorFlow, PyTorch, or Darknet). Follow the installation instructions in the framework's documentation for ARM-based platforms such as the Orange Pi.
3
Download YOLOv8x:

Clone the repository containing the YOLOv8x model from its original source (such as GitHub). You can clone the repository using Git or by downloading it as a zip file and extracting it.
4
Configuration and Compilation:

Navigate to the directory that contains the YOLOv8x code. Follow the repository's instructions to configure the model for your individual hardware and dataset, if necessary. Compile the model in accordance with the repository instructions, which may include running scripts or commands to construct and optimize your system's network architecture.
5
Test and Run the Model:

After successfully constructing the model, test it on sample photos or videos to ensure it functions properly. To run the model on your Orange Pi 5 Plus, execute the accompanying scripts or commands.
6
Optimization and Performance Tuning:

Fine-tune the model's parameters or settings to improve performance or customize it for your individual use case. While the model is running, monitor the system resources (such as CPU and RAM) and make any necessary adjustments.
7
Documentation and Troubleshooting:

Document the installation process and configurations for future reference or sharing. If any problems or failures occur during installation or execution, refer to the model's documentation, forums, or community help for troubleshooting.

Once the YOLOv8x model has been trained, it is capable of detecting and localizing minutia details in new fingerprint images with precision. The model processes input fingerprint images by extracting features through convolutional and pooling layers. These features are then used to predict the presence and location of minutiae points by generating bounding boxes around them. The YOLOv8x approach for fingerprint recognition identifies and outlines minutiae points with a set of bounding boxes. Each bounding box provides the spatial coordinates of the minutiae, which can then be used for further processing and analysis, such as matching and verifying against a database of known FPs. This method ensures the effectiveness of automated fingerprint recognition systems in various applications, including biometric identity verification, access control, and law enforcement. YOLOv8x offers a fast and accurate solution for detecting and pinpointing minutia features in fingerprint images, significantly enhancing the reliability and efficiency of fingerprint recognition systems. Fig. 14 a illustrates the computational analysis process for fingerprint identification used in this study. The YOLOv8x object detection technique involves several phases, which are detailed below.
1.
Pre-processing: Using raw fingerprint images for deep learning can significantly reduce accuracy, so pre-processing is essential. To ensure compatibility with the YOLOv8x model, fingerprint images must undergo pre-processing steps such as scaling, normalization, annotation, and binarization. The auto-annotation function is a key objective, used to assess the deep learning algorithm's performance and minimize errors. The loss function, often referred to simply as "loss," is associated with this objective [92]. After pre-processing, the YOLOv8x model processes the image by passing it through network layers to extract features at different scales. The model then generates bounding boxes, confidence scores, and class probabilities for detected objects in the image.
2.
Training Model: The YOLOv8x model is trained using four pre-processed fingerprint images and pre-trained weights, as shown in Fig. 14b. Although fingerprint images produced with the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm³⁺ composite share similar features, such as image size ratio and object size, they differ in terms of illumination. The model comprises a deep learning neural network, typically based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which excels at recognizing features in fingerprint images.
3.
Prediction: Post-processing refines bounding box coordinates and filters out low-confidence detections, as shown in Fig. 14c. Overlapping bounding boxes are often removed, and the most reliable detections are selected through non-maximum suppression (NMS). The YOLOv8x model outputs a list of identified fingerprint features with their bounding boxes and class labels, which can be analyzed, displayed, or used for further tasks like object tracking, recognition, or classification.

Due to challenges like overlap, dense distribution, small size, and large quantity, fingerprint images are not ideal for direct feature recognition with the YOLOv8x model. To overcome these challenges, this study modifies the YOLOv8x architecture in three significant ways: Replaces the original 32-fold down-sampling large-size feature fusion layer with an 8-fold down-sampling small feature fusion layer, Adjusts the FPN and PAN structures following network pruning and chooses appropriate SPP pooling kernel parameters to reduce the input image size by a factor of 8 in the convolutional layer, resulting in a 13 × 13 activation map from a 416 × 416 input image. To maintain proper activation map dimensions, the input images are scaled to 608 × 608 or other multiples of 32. Fingerprint images are stored as 608 × 608 JPG files for both training and testing, with the training process consisting of 39 epochs and a batch size of 32. We assess our detection model's performance using precision, recall, and F1 score curves by varying the detection threshold. Additionally, a confusion matrix compares actual versus expected classifications to evaluate model accuracy [93,94]. The confusion matrix categorizes results into four key classifications: True Positive (TP) for correctly identified positives, True Negative (TN) for correctly identified negatives, False Positive (FP) for negatives incorrectly labeled as positives, and False Negative (FN) for positives incorrectly labeled as negatives. Fig. S4a displays the confusion matrix graphically. Precision, recall, and F1 score are crucial for evaluating model performance, as each metric captures a different aspect of accuracy. Furthermore, performance metrics for the YOLOv8x model include mAP50, mAP50-95, and validation/box loss curves, with the mAP score reflecting the mean average precision across all classes [95]. For object detection, the mAP score differs from multi-label classification evaluation, as it uses the Intersection over Union (IoU) threshold of 0.5 to measure overlap between predicted and ground truth boxes.

The Precision: Precision is the percentage of correctly identified positive outcomes among all the positive results detected by the analysis.

Mean Average Precision (mAP) is a key metric for evaluating computer vision models. It averages the Average Precision values across all classes to provide a comprehensive performance assessment. A higher mAP score, ranging from 0 to 1, signifies better model performance with lower false negative and false positive rates, reflecting improved recall and precision.
(16)

i = 1
N
A
P i
False positives (FP), true positives (TP), false negatives (FN), precision (P), recall (R), and average precision (AP) are the acronyms for these concepts.

Particularly for the YOLOv8x model, the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric is an essential part of object detection. As seen in Fig. S4b, it assesses the degree to which the projected bounding boxes match the annotated Ground Truth bounding boxes. By calculating the amount of overlap between these boxes, IoU provides a numerical evaluation of how well the model defines object boundaries. The IoU threshold is a crucial machine learning parameter that can be changed to influence whether bounding boxes are classified as True Positives (TP). The minimal overlap required for a bounding box to be regarded as a positive detection is specified by the IoU threshold. As seen in Fig. S4c, an IoU value of 0.5 is typically employed, which means that for a classification to be deemed positive, more than half of the area must overlap between the predicted and actual bounding boxes. The model's performance is significantly affected by changing the IoU threshold. By raising the IoU threshold, the model demands that bounding boxes line more precisely with the Ground Truth in order for them to be categorized as True Positives (TP). This modification may result in a drop in recall as the model's requirements for positive classifications tighten, but it can also increase the model's precision by lowering prediction errors.

For 90 epochs, we use marked fingerprint images to train the model. Up to five bounding boxes can be predicted by each grid cell in the 7x7 region. Both the bifurcation and the ridge end features are detected by the model accurately. The bifurcation feature is conceptually represented in Fig. 15 a, where the red highlights correspond to the YOLOv8 x training results. A location where one ridge divides into two is known as a bifurcation. Variations in the angles and lengths of bifurcations, and their occasional intersections, complicate machine learning. Ridge-ends, the endpoints of lines, are shown in Fig. 15b with training results highlighted in red. Due to their small size and high quantity, Ridge-ends present a challenge for machine learning, as annotating each one is labor-intensive and their high density can affect model performance. To address this, fingerprint images are binarized to simplify features and improve recognition accuracy. Results show that Bifurcation and Ridge-end features both achieve Precision and Recall values of approximately 70 %, which is sufficient for reliable fingerprint recognition. Confusion matrices for these features are presented in Fig. 15a & b. In training, both features initially show a value of 0. Among labeled examples, bifurcation features have an 88 % correct identification rate, while ridge-end features have an 87 % recognition rate. Misclassified features are labeled as background due to the absence of a specific background feature.

The precision, recall, mAP50, and mAP50-95 scores for the bifurcation and ridge end features in a single fingerprint pattern are shown in Fig. 15c–f and 15g-j, respectively. Our study's results demonstrate the attainment of cutting-edge (SOTA) mean average precision (mAP@50) with noteworthy results. In particular, our model performs exceptionally well, as seen by mAP@50 scores of 0.91 for ridge-end feature recognition and 0.93 for bifurcation. These results highlight the efficiency of the YOLOv8x algorithm improved by data augmentation techniques by showing notable improvements in the identification and delineation of bifurcation and ridge end features within fingerprint datasets. In a similar vein, the suggested model successfully identifies scar and loop features. A conceptual depiction of the loop feature is shown in Fig. 15k, where the red dots on the training data for YOLOv8x indicate the points at which a line circles back on itself. Machine learning is hampered by variations in the loops' lengths and angles as well as by their sporadic crossings with other lines. The scar feature notion, represented by line discontinuities in Fig. 15l–is highlighted in red by the training results. Scars are challenging to identify due to their small size and high frequency. Precision and Recall values for loop and scar features are around 70 %, which is sufficient for fingerprint recognition. Fig. 15k & l shows the confusion matrices for these features. During training, both features initially have a value of 0. Scar features achieve a high identification accuracy of 99 %, while loop features have an 83 % recognition rate. Misclassified features are labeled as background due to the lack of a distinct background feature. The precision, recall, mAP50, and mAP50-95 scores for loop and scar features, respectively, throughout different epochs are shown in Fig. 15m-p & q-t. The outcomes show that our model performs notably well and reaches state-of-the-art (SOTA) mean average precision (mAP@50). In particular, the model demonstrates exceptional rates of precision, with mAP@50 scores of 0.99 for scar features and 0.95 for loop features. With the help of data augmentation approaches, the YOLOv8x algorithm has proven to be effective at reliably recognizing and characterizing loop and scar characteristics within fingerprint datasets. These results mark a substantial advancement in the field.

The bifurcation and ridge end feature results over 90 epochs are shown in Fig. S5. These graphs show how our adjusted technique performed throughout different epochs in relation to the baseline (the original YOLOv8x). Precision and recall curves should converge to one for the best fingerprint identification. The curves with our YOLOv8x model demonstrate that in intermediate stages, precision, recall, and mAP approach values that are around 0.7. This suggests that compared to the previous strategy, the upgraded method has better detection performance. After 90 training rounds, the box loss (box_loss), classification loss (cls_loss), and distribution focal loss (dfl_loss) of the enhanced FP-YOLO basic model are displayed in Fig. S5. The detection results of the YOLOv8x model on the validation set are presented in Figs. S5(a, b, g, h). The enhanced model demonstrates reduced missed and false detections, improved feature detection, and higher confidence levels. Fig. S5(c-f, i-l) display the precision-confidence, recall-confidence, precision-recall, and F1-confidence curves, indicating the model's performance at various confidence levels, with a confidence level of 1 representing optimal performance. Additionally, Fig. S6(a-f, g-l) show performance metrics and loss function curves, including the F1-confidence, recall-confidence, precision-recall, and precision-confidence curves for loop and scar features. The labels of recognized features are shown in Figs. S7(a, c, e, & g), providing a visual representation of the detected features and the labels that correlate to them. The bounding boxes in these figures indicate the identified bifurcation, ridge end, loop, and scar locations. A confidence score ranging from 0 to 1 and a unique identifier are appended to each bounding box, demonstrating the accuracy of the YOLOv8x model in recognizing these features. The bounding boxes precisely align with the right fingerprint image attributes, indicating the model's efficacy. Furthermore, the figures demonstrate the YOLOv8x models durability, as it reliably detects a variety of attributes despite changes in image quality or structure. This validates the identified points' practical usability for creating safe keys by showing that the model has successfully extended from the training data to handle a variety of fingerprint picture forms. The correlogram of the labels is shown in Figs. S7(b, d, f, & h), providing a thorough visual depiction of the connections and correlations between the identified features in the fingerprint image. The intensity and direction of the correlations between the variables are shown by the correlation coefficients, which have a range of −1 to 1. Shades of blue are commonly used to represent positive correlations, which indicate that when one variable rises, the other also tends to rise. Darker colours indicate stronger correlations, and the intensity of the colour represents the correlation's strength. A comprehensive visual study of the relationships between the identified bifurcation, ridge end, loop, and scar sites within the fingerprint image is provided by the correlogram displayed in Figs. S7(b, d, f, & h). It draws attention to the strength and direction of these connections, confirming the accuracy of the annotations and the efficacy of the model. It also provides insightful information that can enhance the security key generation process's implementation.

To sum up, the visualization of FPs taken in 365 nm UV light emphasizes how crucial it is to use deep learning methods for feature extraction and dimensionality reduction in order to increase experimental accuracy. When FPs are created using the 5 wt%CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composite, the YOLOv8x model performs exceptionally well, producing images that are more accurate and contrast-rich at 365 nm UV exposure when compared to alternative techniques. While the present fingerprint preparation technique is very successful, a more sophisticated real-time feature identification approach is required. This demand is met by our suggested YOLOv8x deep learning model, which predicts bounding box coordinates and class probabilities with extreme speed and accuracy. Furthermore, we demonstrate the superiority of our deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) in bifurcation and ridge end feature prediction. By means of stringent training and pre-processing methods like binarization, our model exhibits robust performance and can detect errors with success. The training results' efficacy and accuracy attest to our models capacity to recognize important fingerprint characteristics. All things considered, this work advances forensic and identification applications by providing a reliable and effective method for fingerprint analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in anti-counterfeiting of 5 wt%CDs@SAO:Sm3+/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent films have garnered significant interest due to their distinctive properties and potential uses in optical displays, sensors, and anti-counterfeiting technologies. PVA is particularly valued for its strong chemical resistance and excellent film-forming abilities, making it an ideal dielectric polymer for these films [96]. Fig. 16 a illustrates that the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺/PVA composite fluorescent ink emits a vivid orange-red light when exposed to 365 nm UV radiation. Additionally, Fig. 16b reveals the composite's colloidal characteristics and uniform dispersion, which is visible under a red laser light source. The ink mixture is then used to create a clear composite film in a mold. Fig. 16c shows images of the composite films under both UV and visible light excitation. Under 365 nm UV light, the composite film displays intense orange-red fluorescence and excellent optical transparency in visible light. Additionally, the 5 wt% CDs@SAO:Sm³⁺/PVA composite films demonstrate superior processing performance, flexibility, and foldability. Fig. 16d illustrates a simple method for applying an anti-counterfeiting code using a design pattern, showing how to create a UV-stimulated hidden pattern film with a safety mixed ink label. Overall, these results suggest that the composite film has significant potential for anti-counterfeiting applications and can serve as an effective, easy-to-implement solution [97].

In conclusion, the orange-red SAO:Sm3+ phosphor is synthesized via the solution combustion method, while CDs generated by the microwave method are merged into the optimized SAO:7Sm3+ phosphor. The phosphor is phase-pure, and the uniform distribution of the particles is verified by PXRD and SEM measurements. The SAO:Sm3+ phosphors elemental composition and chemical valence states are verified by XPS examination. The samples' emission spectra showed four peaks under 404 nm excitation: at 563 nm, 601 nm, 645 nm, and 706 nm. The highest strong orange-red emission is seen at 645 nm. When comparing the optimized 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composite to the SAO:7Sm3+ phosphor alone, the PL intensity of the latter is increased by 18.2 folds. The 5 wt% CDs@SAO:7Sm3+ composite exhibits exceptional thermal stability and a quantum yield (QY) of 78.3 %. Successful development has produced a w-LED with a high CRI of Ra = 92, a CCT of 5658 K, and CIE coordinates of (0.329, 0.335). The level I–III fingerprint traits, such as ridge patterns, minutiae, and fine details like pores and scars, are successfully revealed by the powder dusting technique. By offering dependable and thorough visualization, this method greatly raises the precision and comprehensiveness of forensic fingerprint analysis. YOLOv8x software sophisticated real-time object detection capabilities show great promise for fingerprint minutiae detection. The accuracy and speed of forensic fingerprint analysis are improved by its accuracy in locating and detecting minutia details as well as its efficiency in handling complex and varied fingerprint images. Furthermore, the results show that SAO:7Sm3+ phosphors are adaptable and appropriate for a range of uses, such as flexible films, LFP detection and w-LEDs.

Krithika: Visualization. Liza Mohapatra: Methodology. R. Pappa Ammal: Formal analysis. Satish Kumar: Validation. K. Manjunatha: Validation, Software. Sheng Yun Wu: Investigation. Shih Lung Yu: Software. H. Nagabhushana: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
